title: Weather journal page design
subtitle: A simple daily tracking layout for temperature, cloud, and conditions
excerpt: A weather journal records daily observations in a fixed format, temperature high and low, cloud cover, precipitation, and a short note. The value of the record is in its consistency: the same layout on every page, filled in at the same time each day, produces a year of comparable data. This tutorial covers designing a minimal but complete daily layout and a monthly overview chart.
sourceNotes: Weather observation journals have been kept by naturalists and amateur meteorologists since at least the seventeenth century. The format used here follows the general conventions documented in Gilbert White's naturalist journals (pre-1800) and the Stevenson screen observation records of the nineteenth century, fixed daily slots with temperature, conditions, and notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The unit of cloud cover used in weather observation. Measured on a scale of 0 (clear sky) to 8 (fully overcast). A quick sketch of a circle filled to the appropriate fraction is the standard shorthand in field journals.

Standardised graphical symbols used on weather maps and in observation records to represent weather conditions, sun, cloud types, rain, snow, fog, and so on. A small set of 8 to 10 symbols covers the vast majority of daily conditions.

A temperature value calculated as the mean of the last 7 or 14 days' readings. Plotting a running average smooths out day-to-day variation and shows seasonal trends more clearly than plotting individual daily readings.

content.text: A weather journal's value is cumulative. A single day's entry tells you little; a year's entries reveal season patterns and local climate tendencies that no published dataset records for your exact location. The layout needs to be complete enough to be useful, but quick enough to fill in every day.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, or notebook pages

content.text: A minimal daily weather entry needs five fields. In order of observational speed:
Date line, three characters: 01 Jan, 14 Mar etc.
circle drawn as a fraction filled in: empty circle = 0, half-filled = 4, fully filled = 8.
Notes, one line of free text for anything unusual.

On the facing page, draw a simple dot-and-line graph plotting daily high temperature across the month. Use a scale of -10 to +30°C on the vertical axis (or narrow this to your local range). Plotting the
as a second line in a different colour shows the seasonal trend clearly.

items.symptom: The journal is abandoned after a few weeks
items.cause: The daily entry takes too long; the format is too detailed.
items.fix: Strip the format down to three fields maximum (date, temperature, one symbol). The quickest version takes under 30 seconds to fill in and is more likely to be maintained.

items.symptom: Temperature data is inconsistent, different times of day on different days
items.cause: No fixed observation time was set.
items.fix: Observe at the same time every day. Morning (before the daily high) and evening (after the daily high) are the two most common. Fix one and stick to it.
